input.command:
cd /tmp && python3 - << 'EOF'
import yaml
d=yaml.safe_load(open('/tmp/ray-client.yaml'))
d.pop('status',None)
m=d['metadata']
for k in ['creationTimestamp','resourceVersion','uid','managedFields','ownerReferences','generation']:
    m.pop(k,None)
m.get('annotations',{}).pop('kubectl.kubernetes.io/last-applied-configuration',None)
# strip nodeName etc for reschedule safety but keep
sp=d['spec']
for k in ['nodeName']:
    sp.pop(k,None)
yaml.safe_dump(d,open('/tmp/ray-client-clean.yaml','w'))
print("ok")
EOF
kubectl -n ray delete pod ray-client --wait=true
kubectl apply -f /tmp/ray-client-clean.yaml

content.text: The script now uses `ray-head:6379` but connecting to the GCS port makes the client try to bring up a local raylet, which fails to determine its node IP. Let me investigate the cluster setup.

input.command:

echo "=== pods ==="; kubectl -n ray get pods -o wide
echo "=== ray-client env RAY ==="; kubectl -n ray exec ray-client -- sh -c 'env | grep -i ray; echo "podIP=$(hostname -i)"; ray --version'

content.text: The service only exposes 6379 and 8265 — there is no 10001 client-server port, which is why the original `ray://ray-head:10001` was the wrong address. Connecting via 6379 makes the pod start a local node, which fails to auto-detect its IP. Let me test fixes interactively.

input.command:
kubectl -n ray exec ray-client -- python -c '
import ray
ray.init(address="ray-head:6379", _node_ip_address="10.244.0.225")
print("pong")
ray.shutdown()
' 2>&1 | tail -20; echo "EXIT=${PIPESTATUS[0]}"

content.text: A driver must run on an actual cluster node (raylet). The client pod has none, so it must first join the cluster as a node. Let me verify that approach works.

input.command:
kubectl -n ray exec ray-client -- bash -c '
ray start --address=ray-head:6379 --node-ip-address=$(hostname -i) >/tmp/raystart.log 2>&1
sleep 5
python -c "import ray; ray.init(address=\"ray-head:6379\"); print(\"pong\"); ray.shutdown()"
rc=$?
ray stop >/dev/null 2>&1
exit $rc
' 2>&1 | tail -15; echo "EXIT=${PIPESTATUS[0]}"

content.text: That works — the pod must join as a cluster node first. I'll make the job script self-contained: attach the pod as a node, then connect to `ray-head:6379`.

input.command:
cat > /tmp/cm.yaml << 'EOF'
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: ray-job-script
  namespace: ray
data:
  job.py: |
    import socket
    import subprocess

    import ray

    # The head service exposes the GCS at ray-head:6379. A driver that talks to
    # the GCS must run on a node that belongs to the cluster, so first attach
    # this pod as a Ray node (idempotent; ignored if already started).
    node_ip = socket.gethostbyname(socket.gethostname())
    subprocess.run(
        ["ray", "start", "--address=ray-head:6379",
         "--node-ip-address={}".format(node_ip)],
        check=False, capture_output=True,
    )

    ray.init(address="ray-head:6379")
    print("pong")
    ray.shutdown()
EOF
kubectl apply -f /tmp/cm.yaml

kubectl -n ray delete pod ray-client --wait=true
kubectl apply -f /tmp/ray-client-clean.yaml
kubectl -n ray wait --for=condition=Ready pod/ray-client --timeout=120s
input.description: Recreate pod to pick up new script

description: Recreate pod to pick up new script
